sections.title: What reported speech does (core logic)
sections.content:
Reported speech (or **indirect speech**) is how we **repeat what someone said** without quoting them word-for-word. We embed their words inside our own sentence, and adjust the tense, pronouns and time references to match our new point of view.

**Direct**: "I'm tired," he said.
**Reported**: He said (that) he was tired.

The word **that** is optional. The reporting verb (*said*, *told*, *asked*) sits in the main clause; the reported words sit in an embedded clause.

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: Reported speech sits in the Clause Linking root (#4 in the Tree Model) — the reported part is **embedded as a subordinate clause**, the same engineering as relative clauses and conditionals. The Verb Phrase root (#2) is secondary because the embedding triggers a **tense shift** (backshift). It's everywhere: telling someone what your boss said, writing news or stories, reporting conversations in exams (B2 sentence transformation), formal emails (*She mentioned that…*).

**Czech-speaker note**: Czech does NOT backshift tenses the same way English does. Direct translation gives *He said he is tired* — a common B1/B2 error. The English engine is different: when the reporting verb goes past, the reported tense usually goes back one step too.

sections.title: The backshift rule (statements)
sections.content:
When the reporting verb is in the **past** (*said*, *told*, *asked*), the tense inside the reported clause usually **shifts one step back in time**.

**The standard shifts**:
- present simple → past simple (*am/is/are* → *was/were*)
- present continuous → past continuous
- present perfect → past perfect
- past simple → past perfect (in careful/formal use)
- **will** → **would**
- **can** → **could**
- **may** → **might**
- **must** → **had to**

**Why it matters**: This is the single highest-impact rule in the topic and the one Czech speakers most often skip. Getting it right makes you sound like a competent reporter; missing it sounds like a literal translation. It's tested in every B1/B2 sentence-transformation exercise.

**Contrast examples**:
- "I **like** coffee." → She said she **liked** coffee.
- "I **am working**." → He said he **was working**.
- "I **have seen** it." → She said she **had seen** it.
- "I **will help**." → He said he **would help**.
- "I **must go**." → She said she **had to** go.

sections.title: When NOT to backshift
sections.content:
Backshift is the default, but not automatic. **Don't** backshift when:

- The reported statement is a **universal truth** or still true now.
  - *She said the Earth **orbits** the sun.* (not *orbited* — still true)
  - *He told me he **lives** in Prague.* (and he still does)
- The **reporting verb is present**: *says*, *tells*, *is saying* — no shift needed.
  - *She **says** she **is** busy.* (no backshift; reporting verb is present)
- You're reporting something **just said**, where the situation hasn't changed.
  - *He just said he **wants** to come.* (still wants it now)

**Why it matters**: This is the **overshoot trap** for Czech speakers. Once the backshift rule is learned, the temptation is to apply it everywhere — even to universal truths or situations that haven't changed. Both extremes (no shift at all / shift everything) sound wrong.

**Contrast**:
- "Water boils at 100°C." → The teacher said water **boils** at 100°C. (truth — no shift)
- "I'm a doctor." → He said he **was** a doctor. (default backshift) **OR** He said he **is** a doctor. (if still true and you want to emphasise that)

sections.title: Said vs told (and other reporting verbs)
sections.content:
The two most common reporting verbs split on **who comes next**:

- **say** does NOT take a person object directly. Either no person (*She said that…*) or with **to** (*She said to me that…*).
- **tell** REQUIRES a person object. *She told me that…* (never *She told that…*)

**Quick test**: *tell me*, *tell him*, *tell us* — yes. *say me*, *say him* — no.

**Other useful reporting verbs**:
- **asked** — for questions and requests
- **replied** — answered
- **mentioned** — said briefly
- **explained** — said with reasoning
- **promised** — committed
- **warned** — alerted to danger
- **admitted** — confessed
- **suggested** — proposed

**Why it matters**: *say* vs *tell* is one of the most common Czech-speaker mistakes (*"He said me…"* is a high-frequency B1 error). Variety in reporting verbs is a B2 writing-and-speaking marker — using only *said* the whole time flattens your English.

**Contrast**:
- He **said** (that) he was tired. ✓
- He **told me** (that) he was tired. ✓
- He **told** (that) he was tired. ✗ (no person)
- He **said me** (that) he was tired. ✗ (no person object with *say*)

sections.title: Reported questions
sections.content:
When we report a question, three things change:

1. **No question mark** at the end (the whole sentence is now a statement).
2. **No *do/does/did*** — drop the auxiliary used to form the question.
3. **Word order goes back to statement order**: SUBJECT + VERB (not VERB + SUBJECT).

**Yes/No questions**: introduce with **if** or **whether**.
- "Are you busy?" → She asked **if/whether** I **was** busy.
- "Do you like coffee?" → He asked **if** I **liked** coffee.

**Wh- questions**: keep the wh-word (*where, when, what, why, who, how*), then statement order.
- "Where do you live?" → He asked **where** I **lived**. (not *where do I live*)
- "What time is it?" → She asked **what time it was**. (not *what time is it*)

**Why it matters**: This is the second classic Czech-speaker trap (after backshift). Czech keeps question word order in indirect questions; English doesn't. Getting *He asked where I lived* (not *where do I live*) is a clear B1/B2 marker, and it's tested constantly.

**Contrast**:
- "Where are you going?" → He asked **where I was going**. ✓ (not *where was I going*)
- "Can you swim?" → She asked **if I could swim**. ✓ (not *if could I swim*)

sections.title: Reported commands and requests
sections.content:
Commands and requests use a completely different pattern from statements — no *that*-clause, no backshift. The structure is:

**tell / ask + PERSON + (not) to-infinitive**

- "Close the door." → She **told me to close** the door.
- "Please wait." → He **asked me to wait**.
- "Don't worry." → She **told him not to worry**. (negative: *not* before *to*)

**tell** vs **ask** signals tone:
- **tell** = instruction or order (*She told me to leave.*)
- **ask** = request, more polite (*She asked me to leave.*)

Other verbs in the same pattern: *order, warn, advise, invite, remind, beg*.
- *The police **ordered** them **to stop**.*
- *He **warned** me **not to go**.*
- *She **reminded** me **to call**.*

**Why it matters**: Commands appear all the time in real reporting — instructions at work, parental rules, exam scenarios. Czech speakers often try *He said me to close the door* (wrong on two counts: *said me* and the said+to-inf pattern). The fix: *tell* or *ask* + person + to-infinitive.

**Contrast**:
- "Sit down." → He **told me to sit** down. ✓
- "Sit down." → He **said to me sit** down. ✗
- "Don't be late." → She **told me not to be** late. ✓ (negative pattern)

sections.title: Pronoun, time and place shifts
sections.content:
Backshift is only one layer. When the speaker, time and place change, the referring words change too.

**Pronouns** shift to match the new speaker:
- *I* → *he / she*, *my* → *his / her*, *we* → *they*, *you* → *I / me / he / she*
- "**I** lost **my** keys." → She said **she** had lost **her** keys.

**Time words** shift to match the new time:
- *now* → *then*, *today* → *that day*, *tonight* → *that night*
- *yesterday* → *the day before / the previous day*
- *tomorrow* → *the next day / the following day*
- *last week* → *the week before*, *next week* → *the following week*
- *ago* → *before*

**Place / demonstratives** shift to match the new place:
- *here* → *there*
- *this* → *that*, *these* → *those*

**Why it matters**: Forgetting these makes the report incoherent: *He said he saw her yesterday* means HE saw her yesterday from your point of view today — but if he said it last week, you need *the day before*. Time/place shifts are also high-yield in exam transformations.

**Example combining all three**:
- Direct: "**I** met **your** brother **here yesterday**."
- Reported (told to you, much later): She said **she** had met **my** brother **there the day before**.

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors + roadmap
sections.content:
**High-frequency mistakes** to watch for:

- **No backshift**: *He said he **is** tired.* → *He said he **was** tired.*
- **Over-backshift on truths**: *She said water **boiled** at 100°C.* → *She said water **boils** at 100°C.* (universal truth — no shift)
- **Question word order in reported questions**: *He asked where **do I live**.* → *He asked where **I lived**.*
- **Tell without a person**: *She **told that** she would come.* → *She **told me** that…* OR *She **said** that…*
- **Say with a person object**: *He **said me** he was busy.* → *He **told me** he was busy.* OR *He **said to me** he was busy.*
- **Said + to-infinitive for commands**: *He **said me to close** the door.* → *He **told me to close** the door.*

**Why it matters**: These six errors cover roughly 90% of mistakes Czech B1/B2 learners make in reported speech. Spotting and fixing them gives a visible jump in accuracy.

**Direct practice in this curriculum** (start here after the cards):
- Guided MC (backshift, *say/tell*, question order)
- Gap fill (transformations from direct to reported)
- Error correction (fix the Czech-speaker trap sentences)

**Next steps**: For the underlying tense forms (past simple, past perfect, past continuous), see the [[topic:tenses_general|Tenses: General]] topic. Reported speech sits next to [[topic:conditionals|Conditionals]] and [[topic:relative_pronouns|Relative Pronouns]] in the Clause Linking root — same embedding logic, different shape.

questions.question: "I am leaving tomorrow." Which reported sentence is correct?

options.a: She said she was leaving the next day.
options.b: She said she is leaving tomorrow.
options.c: She said she was leaving tomorrow.
options.d: She said she had left the next day.

questions.explanation: Backshift: am leaving → was leaving; tomorrow → the next day.

questions.question: "Where do you live?" → He asked …
questions.explanation: Reported question: statement order (where I lived) and past backshift.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?

options.a: She told me that she would come.
options.b: She told that she would come.
options.c: She said me that she would come.
options.d: She told to me that she would come.

questions.explanation: Tell + person (tell me). Say does not take indirect object without 'to'.

questions.question: "Close the window." → She …

options.a: said me to close the window
options.c: told me to close the window
options.d: told to me close the window

questions.explanation: Reported command: tell + person + to-infinitive.

questions.question: "I have finished my work." → He said …
questions.explanation: Present perfect → past perfect in reported speech: had finished.

questions.question: "Are you coming?" → She asked me …
questions.explanation: Yes/No reported question: if + statement order (if I was coming).

questions.question: "I am tired." → She said she _____ tired.
questions.explanation: Backshift: present (am) → past (was) in reported speech.

questions.question: "We will help." → They said they _____ help.
questions.explanation: Will → would in reported speech.

questions.question: "Where do you live?" → He asked me where I _____.
questions.explanation: Reported question: statement order; present → past (lived).

questions.question: "I have seen the film." → She said she _____ seen the film.
questions.explanation: Present perfect → past perfect (had seen).

questions.question: "Close the door." → He _____ me to close the door.
questions.explanation: Reported command: tell + person + to-infinitive.

questions.question: "I can swim." → He said he _____ swim.
questions.explanation: Can → could in reported speech.

questions.question: "What is your name?" → She asked what my name _____.
questions.explanation: Reported question: what my name was (statement order).

questions.question: "I saw her yesterday." → He said he had seen her _____.
questions.explanation: Yesterday → the day before in reported speech.

questions.question: "Are you ready?" → He asked me _____ I was ready.
questions.explanation: Yes/No reported questions use **if** or whether.

questions.question: "I must go now." → She said she _____ to go then.
questions.explanation: Must → had to; now → then.

questions.question: "I am tired." → She said she _____ tired.
questions.explanation: Present → past: am → was.

questions.question: "We will help." → They said they _____ help.
questions.explanation: Will → would in reported speech.

questions.question: "Where do you live?" → He asked me where I _____.
questions.explanation: Reported question: statement order; present → past.

questions.question: "I have seen the film." → She said she _____ seen the film.
questions.explanation: Present perfect → past perfect: had seen.

questions.question: "Close the door." → He _____ me to close the door.
questions.explanation: Reported command: tell + person + to-infinitive.

questions.question: "I can swim." → He said he _____ swim.
questions.explanation: Can → could in reported speech.

questions.question: "What is your name?" → She asked (me) what my name _____.
questions.explanation: Reported question: what my name was (statement order).

questions.question: "I saw her yesterday." → He said he had seen her _____.
questions.explanation: Yesterday → the day before in reported speech.

questions.question: "Are you ready?" → He asked me _____ I was ready.
questions.explanation: Yes/No reported: if (or whether) + statement order.

questions.question: "I must go now." → She said she _____ to go then.
questions.explanation: Must → had to; now → then.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She said she is tired.' (reporting past words) Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Backshift: is → was when reporting in the past.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He asked where do I live.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Reported question: statement order (where I lived).

questions.question: Wrong: 'She told that she would come.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She told me that she would come.
She said that she would come.
She told me that she would come
She said that she would come
questions.explanation: Tell needs a person: tell me. Or use said (that).

questions.question: Wrong: 'He said me to close the door.' Correct the sentence.
answers: He told me to close the door.
He told me to close the door
questions.explanation: Commands use tell + person + to-infinitive, not say.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She said she has finished.' (reporting past words) Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Present perfect → past perfect: had finished.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He asked if am I coming.' Correct the sentence.
answers: He asked if I was coming.
He asked if I was coming
questions.explanation: Yes/No reported: if + statement order (if I was coming).

questions.question: Wrong: 'She said to me that she will help.' Correct the sentence. We are reporting something that was said in the past, so change the tense of the verb in the that-clause. (You may keep 'said to me' or use 'told me'.)
answers: She said to me that she would help.
She told me that she would help.
She said to me that she would help
She told me that she would help
questions.explanation: Will → would when reporting in the past.

questions.question: Wrong: 'They asked what time is it.' Correct the sentence.
answers: They asked what time it was.
They asked what time it was
questions.explanation: Reported question: statement order (what time it was).
